title: Matter and Separation
subtitle: Pure and impure substances, mixtures, solutions and separation techniques.

studyGuide.heading: Pure substances and mixtures
studyGuide.html:
• A pure substance contains only one type of substance (one element or one compound), e.g. distilled water.
  • A mixture contains two or more substances not chemically joined, so they can be separated by physical means.
  • A pure substance melts and boils at a fixed temperature; a mixture melts/boils over a range.

studyGuide.heading: Key words for solutions
studyGuide.html:
• Solvent — the liquid that does the dissolving (e.g. water).
  • Solute — the solid that dissolves (e.g. salt).
  • Solution — solute + solvent mixed together (e.g. salt water).
  • Soluble — can dissolve; insoluble — cannot dissolve.
  • Saturated solution — no more solute can dissolve at that temperature.
studyGuide.mnemonic: The solute is the solid that disappears; the solvent is the liquid that does the dissolving. (solute = the smaller amount.)

studyGuide.heading: Filtration
studyGuide.html:
Separates an insoluble solid from a liquid.

  • The solid stays on the filter paper — this is the residue.
  • The liquid that passes through is the filtrate.
  • Example: sand from water.

diagram.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 170' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.s{stroke:#1f2a44;stroke-width:2;fill:none}.t{font:10px Nunito;fill:#1f2a44}</style><path class='s' d='M70 20 L150 20 L110 70 Z'/><path d='M70 20 L150 20 L130 45 L90 45 Z' fill='#f0c27a'/><path class='s' d='M85 100 L95 150 L155 150 L165 100 Z'/><line class='s' x1='110' y1='70' x2='110' y2='100'/><circle cx='110' cy='90' r='2' fill='#5b8def'/><circle cx='110' cy='98' r='2' fill='#5b8def'/><text class='t' x='160' y='35'>residue</text><text class='t' x='168' y='130'>filtrate</text></svg>
diagram.caption: Insoluble solid (residue) stays in the paper; liquid (filtrate) drips through.

studyGuide.heading: Evaporation and crystallisation
studyGuide.html:
Separates a soluble solid from its solvent.

  • Heat the solution so the solvent evaporates, leaving the solid behind.
  • For nice crystals, heat gently to a saturated solution then let it cool slowly (crystallisation).
  • Example: getting salt from salt water.

studyGuide.heading: Distillation
studyGuide.html:
• Simple distillation separates a solvent (pure liquid) from a solution. The liquid evaporates, passes through a condenser where it cools back to liquid, and is collected. Example: pure water from salt water.
  • Fractional distillation separates a mixture of liquids with different boiling points (e.g. crude oil, or ethanol from water). The liquid with the lowest boiling point evaporates first.

diagram.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 300 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.s{stroke:#1f2a44;stroke-width:2;fill:none}.t{font:10px Nunito;fill:#1f2a44}</style><path class='s' d='M30 60 a25 25 0 0 0 50 0 L80 30 L30 30 Z'/><path d='M34 60 a21 21 0 0 0 42 0 Z' fill='#cdeafd'/><line class='s' x1='55' y1='30' x2='55' y2='15'/><line class='s' x1='55' y1='15' x2='180' y2='15'/><line class='s' x1='180' y1='15' x2='230' y2='95'/><rect x='150' y='5' width='90' height='20' class='s' transform='rotate(28 195 15)'/><path class='s' d='M225 95 a18 18 0 0 0 36 0 L261 80 L225 80 Z'/><path d='M229 95 a14 14 0 0 0 28 0 Z' fill='#cdeafd'/><text class='t' x='110' y='55'>vapour →</text><text class='t' x='150' y='75'>condenser</text><text class='t' x='225' y='130'>pure liquid</text></svg>
diagram.caption: Evaporate, then cool the vapour in a condenser back to a pure liquid.

studyGuide.mnemonic: Distillation keeps the liquid (it drips into the beaker); evaporation keeps the solid (left behind).

studyGuide.heading: Chromatography
studyGuide.html:
• Separates a mixture of coloured substances (e.g. inks, dyes).
  • A spot of ink is placed on paper above the solvent; as the solvent rises, different dyes travel different distances depending on how soluble they are.
  • More soluble dyes travel further up the paper.
  • The pattern of spots is a chromatogram.

diagram.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 250 185' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.s{stroke:#1f2a44;stroke-width:2;fill:none}.t{font:9px Nunito;fill:#1f2a44}.p{font:9px Nunito;fill:#3f6fd1}</style><path class='s' d='M55 110 L55 172 L185 172 L185 110'/><rect x='57' y='150' width='126' height='22' fill='#cdeafd'/><text class='t' x='188' y='166'>solvent</text><rect x='105' y='30' width='34' height='134' fill='#fff' stroke='#1f2a44' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='105' y1='143' x2='139' y2='143' stroke='#1f2a44' stroke-dasharray='3'/><text class='t' x='143' y='146'>start line (pencil)</text><line x1='105' y1='44' x2='139' y2='44' stroke='#6b7790' stroke-dasharray='3'/><text class='t' x='143' y='47'>solvent front</text><ellipse cx='122' cy='143' rx='8' ry='4' fill='#8e44ad'/><ellipse cx='122' cy='116' rx='7' ry='4' fill='#e0524d'/><ellipse cx='122' cy='88' rx='7' ry='4' fill='#2b9d6b'/><ellipse cx='122' cy='62' rx='7' ry='4' fill='#5b8def'/><text class='p' x='150' y='92'>dyes separate</text><text class='p' x='150' y='104'>by solubility</text></svg>
diagram.caption: The dyes in the ink rise with the solvent; the most soluble travels furthest. The start spot must sit above the solvent.

flashcards.back: The liquid that dissolves the solute (e.g. water).
The solid that dissolves in the solvent (e.g. salt).
A solute completely dissolved in a solvent (e.g. salt water).
Soluble = can dissolve; insoluble = cannot dissolve.
A solution that can dissolve no more solute at that temperature.
Contains only one substance; melts/boils at a fixed temperature.
Two or more substances not chemically joined; separated physically.
An insoluble solid from a liquid (e.g. sand from water).
Residue = solid left in the paper; filtrate = liquid that passes through.
A soluble solid from its solvent (e.g. salt from salt water).
A pure liquid (solvent) from a solution (e.g. water from salt water).
Liquids with different boiling points (e.g. crude oil).
Cools the vapour so it condenses back into a liquid.
Coloured substances such as inks and dyes.
Travels the furthest up the paper.
Pure = sharp fixed temperature; impure/mixture = boils over a range.

commonMistakes.title: Swapping solute and solvent
commonMistakes.detail: Solute = the solid that dissolves; solvent = the liquid doing the dissolving.

commonMistakes.title: Saying dissolved salt 'disappears'
commonMistakes.detail: It is still there in the solution — the mass is conserved; it can be recovered by evaporation.

commonMistakes.title: Using filtration for salt water
commonMistakes.detail: Filtration cannot separate a dissolved (soluble) solid — use evaporation instead.

commonMistakes.title: Mixing up distillation and evaporation
commonMistakes.detail: Distillation keeps the liquid; evaporation keeps the solid.

commonMistakes.title: Drawing the chromatography spot below the solvent
commonMistakes.detail: The ink spot must be ABOVE the solvent line, or the ink washes into the solvent.

commonMistakes.title: Calling a mixture 'pure'
commonMistakes.detail: Only one substance = pure. Even clear, clean-looking sea water is a mixture.

questions.q: In salt water, what is the solute?
questions.explanation: Salt is the solid that dissolves — the solute.

questions.q: Which method separates sand from water?
questions.explanation: Sand is insoluble, so filtration removes it.

questions.q: Which method gets pure water FROM salt water?
questions.explanation: Distillation evaporates the water and condenses it back, leaving salt behind.
questions.hint: You want to keep the liquid, not the solid.

questions.q: A substance that can dissolve is described as…
What is the solid left in the filter paper called?
questions.explanation: The residue is the solid trapped by the filter paper.

questions.q: Which technique separates the colours in black ink?
Define a 'pure substance'.
questions.modelAnswer: A pure substance is made of only one type of substance (one element or one compound) and melts and boils at a fixed temperature.
questions.hint: Only one thing in it — and what about its boiling point?
questions.examMistake: Saying 'clean' or 'natural' — pure has a scientific meaning.

questions.q: Name the apparatus that cools vapour back into a liquid during distillation.
questions.examMistake: Don't say 'cooler' — the proper name is the condenser.

questions.q: A saturated solution is one that…
options: Can dissolve no more solute at that temperature
questions.explanation: Saturated = no more solute will dissolve at that temperature.

questions.q: Which is a mixture?
questions.explanation: Sea water contains water plus many dissolved salts — a mixture.
questions.hint: Which has more than one substance?

questions.q: How would you get salt crystals from salt water?
questions.modelAnswer: Heat the salt water so the water evaporates, leaving the salt behind. For crystals, evaporate to a saturated solution then leave it to cool slowly so crystals form.
questions.examMistake: Suggesting filtration — the salt is dissolved, not solid.

questions.q: The liquid that passes through the filter paper is the…
questions.explanation: The filtrate is the liquid that drips through.

questions.q: Explain why filtration cannot be used to separate salt from salt water, but evaporation can.
questions.modelAnswer: Salt is soluble, so its tiny particles are dissolved and spread evenly in the water; they are far too small to be trapped by filter paper, so they pass straight through. Evaporation works because heating removes the water as vapour and leaves the dissolved salt behind as a solid.
questions.hint: Are the salt particles big enough to be trapped?
questions.examMistake: Forgetting that dissolved particles pass through filter paper.

questions.q: In chromatography, why must the ink spot be above the solvent level?
options: So the ink does not dissolve straight into the solvent
questions.explanation: If the spot is below the solvent it washes off into the solvent instead of separating up the paper.
questions.hint: What happens if the spot sits in the liquid?

questions.q: Describe how simple distillation separates pure water from ink.
questions.modelAnswer: The ink solution is heated so the water evaporates into vapour. The vapour passes into the condenser where cold water cools it, so it condenses back into liquid water, which is collected in a beaker. The coloured ink has a much higher boiling point and stays behind in the flask.
questions.examMistake: Forgetting the condenser step or what stays behind.

questions.q: A pure substance melts at exactly 0°C. A sample melts from -3°C to 2°C. What does this tell you?
questions.explanation: Melting over a range (not a sharp point) shows it is impure / a mixture.

questions.q: Explain the difference between a solution being concentrated and being saturated.
questions.modelAnswer: A concentrated solution has a large amount of solute dissolved in the solvent, but you could still dissolve more. A saturated solution has so much solute that no more will dissolve at that temperature; any extra solute stays as solid at the bottom.
questions.examMistake: Using 'concentrated' and 'saturated' as if they mean the same thing.

questions.q: Which separation technique would best separate two liquids that mix together but have different boiling points?
questions.explanation: Fractional distillation separates miscible liquids by their different boiling points.

questions.q: When salt dissolves in water, the mass of the solution equals the mass of the water plus the salt. What does this show?
questions.modelAnswer: It shows that mass is conserved — the salt has not disappeared, its particles are just spread out among the water particles. The salt can be recovered again by evaporating the water.
questions.hint: Where did the salt go if the mass is the same?
questions.examMistake: Saying the salt is destroyed or disappears.

questions.q: Two dyes are tested by chromatography. Dye A travels 8 cm and dye B travels 3 cm in the same time. Which is more soluble?
questions.explanation: More soluble dyes are carried further up the paper, so dye A is more soluble.

questions.q: Why should you heat a solution gently and slowly when crystallising, rather than boiling it dry?
questions.modelAnswer: Heating gently and stopping before it is fully dry, then letting it cool slowly, lets large, regular crystals form. Boiling it dry too fast forms only a tiny powder and can spit hot solid out, which is dangerous and gives poor crystals.
questions.hint: What forms if you cool slowly vs boil dry?
questions.examMistake: Thinking faster heating gives better crystals.

questions.q: Which list contains ONLY pure substances?
questions.explanation: Distilled water, pure copper and oxygen each contain one substance only.

questions.q: A student is given a mixture of sand, salt and water. Describe, in order, how to obtain pure dry salt and pure sand from it.
questions.modelAnswer: First filter the mixture: the sand is insoluble and stays as residue in the filter paper, while the salt solution passes through as filtrate. Wash and dry the sand to get pure sand. Then evaporate the salt solution (the filtrate) by heating so the water leaves as vapour, leaving solid salt behind; dry it to get pure salt. (Distillation could be used instead if you also wanted to keep the water.)
questions.hint: Separate the insoluble part first, then deal with the dissolved part.
questions.examMistake: Trying to evaporate first, which leaves salt and sand mixed together.

questions.q: Explain how fractional distillation separates a mixture of ethanol (boiling point 78°C) and water (100°C).
questions.modelAnswer: The mixture is heated. Ethanol has the lower boiling point so it evaporates first; its vapour rises up the fractionating column. Water vapour mostly condenses on the column and runs back, while ethanol vapour passes into the condenser, cools, condenses and is collected first. As temperature rises toward 100°C, water can then be collected separately.
questions.examMistake: Saying water comes off first — ethanol's boiling point is lower.

questions.q: Why does a mixture not have a fixed melting point, while a pure substance does?
options: The different substances in a mixture melt at different temperatures, giving a range
questions.explanation: Each component melts at its own temperature, so a mixture melts over a range; a pure substance has one fixed point.
questions.hint: Think about each component melting separately.

questions.q: Forensic scientists use chromatography on inks from a ransom note and four pens. Explain how it can identify which pen was used.
questions.modelAnswer: Each ink is a mixture of dyes that separate into a pattern of spots at characteristic heights, depending on each dye's solubility. The note's ink is run alongside the four pens under identical conditions. The pen whose chromatogram has spots at exactly the same heights and colours as the note must contain the same dyes, identifying the pen used.
questions.hint: Compare the spot patterns under the same conditions.
questions.examMistake: Forgetting that the samples must be run under the same conditions to compare.

questions.q: Sea water can be made drinkable by distillation. Explain why this works but is expensive.
questions.modelAnswer: Distillation works because heating the sea water evaporates pure water vapour, leaving the dissolved salts behind; the vapour is condensed back to pure liquid water. It is expensive because a lot of energy (heat) is needed to boil large volumes of water, and energy/fuel costs money.
questions.hint: What stays behind, and why is the energy a problem?
questions.examMistake: Not explaining why energy makes it costly.

questions.q: 50 g of salt is added to water and stirred; only 36 g dissolves and 14 g sits at the bottom. What can you conclude?
options: The solution is saturated at that temperature
questions.explanation: Undissolved solid remaining means no more can dissolve — the solution is saturated.
questions.hint: Why won't the last 14 g dissolve?

questions.q: Describe a fair-test investigation into how temperature affects the mass of sugar that dissolves in 100 cm³ of water.
questions.modelAnswer: Independent variable: water temperature (e.g. 20, 40, 60, 80°C). Dependent variable: mass of sugar that dissolves before saturation. Control variables: same volume of water (100 cm³), same stirring, same type of sugar. Method: heat the water to each temperature, add sugar a little at a time, stirring, until no more dissolves; record the mass added. Repeat each temperature and take a mean, then plot mass against temperature.
questions.hint: Name the variables and how you decide it's saturated.
questions.examMistake: Not controlling the volume of water or the stirring.

questions.q: Explain why distilled water is described as pure but tap water is not, even though both look the same.
questions.modelAnswer: Distilled water has been evaporated and condensed, removing all dissolved substances, so it contains only water — one substance — and is pure. Tap water looks clear but contains dissolved minerals, chlorine and other substances, so it is a mixture and not pure, even though you cannot see the difference.
questions.hint: Looks can deceive — what is dissolved in tap water?
